gaps.title: Hierarchical / nested block storage
gaps.description: pi-project's block-api assumes one flat JSON file per block. There is no way to express a nested decomposition like roadmap → milestone → epic → story → task as a directory tree. Work decomposition must be smuggled into a single flat array with denormalized parent references, which loses scope ownership and forces the global tasks.json to conflate work across all levels.

evidence.reference: readBlock / writeBlock / appendToBlock all take a simple block name and resolve to .project/<name>.json — no subpath support
current tasks block is a flat array with integer phase references; no milestone or epic hierarchy
PROJECT_DIR / SCHEMAS_DIR constants assume flat directory layout

buildIdIndex special-cases .project/phases/ — synthesized IDs from 'number'/'name' fields, not top-level 'id'. First of two existing one-off subdirectory walks that FGAP-001 generalizes; resolution shape is 'generalize two existing walks' rather than 'introduce subdirectory recursion'.

readBlockDir(cwd, subdir) reads all *.json from .project/<subdir>/ — second existing one-off subdirectory walk. Phases-pattern coexistence rule: no .project/phases.json file exists alongside the .project/phases/ directory; partition replaces flat file in current precedent.

Phase-files use synthesized IDs from non-'id' fields. Generalizing the subdirectory-walk pattern requires deciding whether per-subdirectory ID derivation rules are configurable like phases or whether subpath block files always carry top-level 'id'.

gaps.impact:
The L3 Work layer from the Muni five-layer model cannot be natively represented. Features like the consumer migration arc must live as single flat JSON files per feature, with stories and tasks embedded as arrays — preventing per-task atomic read/write and forcing whole-feature reads for any single-task operation.

Per-file proper-lockfile contention: every write to features.json today blocks every other write to features.json. Partition-per-file unblocks parallel writes to siblings — concurrency improvement is an immediately-enabled effect of FGAP-001's resolution.

Migration scope: per analysis/2026-05-01-github-issues-migration-inventory.md, nine blocks have issue-tracker shape and benefit from partitioning — framework-gaps, issues, tasks, spec-reviews, features, requirements, verification, audit, layer-plans. Single-block treatment understates the work envelope.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: hierarchical work breakdown structure (WBS); nested aggregate storage
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Extend block-api to support subpath block names (e.g. readBlock('features/consumer-migration')). Add nested-directory discovery in availableBlocks. Schemas at the subdirectory root apply to all files underneath. Authority for partial-tree invalidation belongs to block-validation.ts.

Schema validation atomicity: partition writes implicitly target schema.properties.{arrayKey}.items as the per-file schema. The schema-walk's target object is the array-item subschema, not a separate schema file. Schema authoring contract changes implicitly with this resolution.

Block-validation rollback: block-validation.ts snapshots .project/*.json before each step and rolls back on failure. Recursion into subdirectories is part of the rollback contract change, not a separate concern.

Defaults disposition: superseded by DEC-0011 (enacted 2026-05-03) — pi-project ships no default schemas or default block files; users install desired schemas via an opt-in mechanism. Bundled defaults retired. The schema-walk semantics change does not propagate via package-shipped defaults.

DEC-0009 enactment update (2026-05-03): closure-table relations are the canonical primitive for hierarchical decomposition. The subpath block-names direction above is superseded — FGAP-001's resolution path is now closure-table-only via a relations.json block kind landing in pi-project. The phases-pattern subdirectory walks remain as legacy exceptions, not the generalized resolution. POC at analysis/poc-degree-zero-lens/ proves the closure-table shape mechanically; production landing requires block-api integration of the new block kind plus SDK consumers (validateRelations, walkDescendants, edgesForLens, groupByLens).

DEC-0009's closure-table-only direction enacted; relations.json + 5 SDK consumers (loadRelations, validateRelations, walkDescendants, edgesForLens, groupByLens) ship in pi-context. gaps.title: Per-scope finding registries
gaps.description: There is no convention for a block to carry its own embedded findings array distinct from global issues.json. A design issue discovered during review of jit-agents-spec.md has nowhere to live except the global issue tracker, where it is indistinguishable from task-level defects and feature-level scope conflicts. The result is issues.json conflating L2 architectural contradictions with L4 implementation bugs with L5 incident-derived rules.

evidence.reference: 62KB flat file mixing spec contradictions, task bugs, and session observations with no scope tagging beyond the informal 'package' field
single flat items array with no parent-scope reference — issues float free of the artifact they belong to

gaps.impact: Every finding discovered during any layer of work lands in the same flat bucket. There is no mechanism to say 'this finding belongs to the review of spec X' or 'this defect belongs to task Y within feature Z'. The global tracker becomes an unmanageable tangle that must be sorted by hand to answer any scoped question.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: scoped issues; per-artifact defect list; embedded finding registries
gaps.proposed_resolution: Standardize a 'findings' sub-array on every L2/L3/L4 block schema kind (specs, features, tasks). Each finding carries scope metadata (layer, parent_artifact). Provide a block-api helper appendFinding(blockName, parentItemId, finding). Global issues.json becomes a derived view (see FGAP-003).

gaps.title: Materialized views over scoped blocks
gaps.description: There is no derived block kind. projectState() does in-memory aggregation but it is not a persisted, addressable, schema-validated block. There is no way to declare 'global issues_view.json is a derived index over per-scope finding registries' or 'open_work_view.json aggregates all non-terminal stories across features'.

evidence.reference: projectState() computes derived metrics at query time but returns in-memory only — no persistence, no addressability, no schema
only writeable blocks exist; no concept of a read-only derived block

gaps.impact: Consumers who want a flat queryable view (the current issues.json shape) cannot have it alongside scope-owned registries without manual sync. Two sources of truth or fragile regeneration.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a view-block kind with a definition file that names source blocks, a projection function, and refresh triggers. block-api exposes readView(name) but disallows direct writes. Refresh fires on underlying block writes. Schemas describe the view shape.

gaps.title: Authorship attestation at write time
gaps.description: Only issues.schema.json has a source enum (human | agent | monitor | workflow). tasks.schema.json has assigned_agent which is a different concept (assignment, not authorship). No block records created_by / modified_by / created_at / modified_at consistently. block-api.ts does not stamp authorship on writes. The authorship-matrix principle — 'only the user can advance a finding from proposed to decided' — cannot be enforced because the writer identity is not recorded.

evidence.reference: source enum exists here and only here — partial coverage, no other block has equivalent fields
writeBlock / appendToBlock / updateItemInBlock do not inject authorship metadata
assigned_agent field is assignment semantics, not authorship — a task can be assigned to agent X but written by user Y

gaps.impact: State-machine validation of user-gated transitions (decision enacted, finding decided, feature approved) is structurally impossible. The framework cannot distinguish 'user advanced this' from 'agent advanced this' and so cannot enforce authority matrices. The spec-loop invariant M4 is unenforceable.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: authorship attestation; written_by attribution; audit trail
gaps.proposed_resolution:
block-api.ts stamps created_by / modified_by / created_at / modified_at on every write, reading writer identity from a DispatchContext parameter. Every block schema adds these as required fields. Cross-block validation can then enforce authority-matrix rules per-transition.

CLOSURE (agent/step-8.5-orchestrator 2026-05-09T23:58:13.827Z): Structurally closed by `001d11a` (Step 3 DispatchContext + WriterIdentity + stampItem) + `2ed2102` (Step 3.1 per-field schema declaration honoring) + `d24769a` (Step 6.2 upsert attestation pre-merge). DispatchContext + WriterIdentity + per-field-aware stampItem land authorship attestation across all block-api write surfaces; upsert pre-merge preserves created_* across replacement updates. Description text above preserves the forensic record of the pre-closure state.

gaps.title: State-machine validation on enum-field transitions
gaps.description: AJV draft-07 validates current-value membership on enum fields but cannot express allowed transitions. Every block schema today permits any enum value on any write. 'Issue status can only go from open to in-progress, not back to open' is not expressible. 'Finding decided-state transitions require user authority' is not expressible.

evidence.reference: status enum has open | resolved | deferred but no transitions constraint — any value can overwrite any other on updateItemInBlock
AJV draft-07 wrapper with no custom keywords; no before-value inspection at write time
updateItemInBlock does not pass the prior value to validation, so transition rules cannot be applied even if expressible

gaps.impact: The whole class of lifecycle-gated workflows (design review clean-gate, decision enacted gate, feature approval gate) cannot be enforced by schema. Current workaround is discipline. Discipline fails silently when agents run unattended.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: state-machine validation; lifecycle invariant; transition guard
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a custom AJV keyword (x-lifecycle) that the schema-validator reads. block-api.ts passes both prior-value and new-value to the validator at update time. Validator checks allowed transitions and required-authority. Fails write if transition is invalid or authority is insufficient.

gaps.title: Schema versioning and evolution
gaps.description: Schemas have no version field, no $id, no migration story, no $ref composition, no schema reuse via $ref. Schemas drift silently across projects and across time. A schema rename or field rename has no backward-compatibility path. Schemas are effectively copy-paste snapshots with no evolution protocol.

evidence.reference: flat draft-07 schema with no $id, no version, no allOf / anyOf composition
duplicates fields from decisions.schema.json with no $ref to a shared schema — rename in one does not propagate
'All schemas are user-customizable. Edit .project/schemas/*.schema.json to add fields, change enums, or restructure — no code changes needed.' — no mention of migration or version compatibility

gaps.impact: Long-term schema evolution is undefined. Projects that adopted the framework early and never updated their .project/schemas/ drift from projects that adopted later. There is no way for tooling to know what schema version a block conforms to.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: schema evolution; JSON Schema $id; schema registry; schema versioning
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Add a required $id (URI) and version field on every schema. schema-validator reads version and can apply migration functions. Introduce a schema-migrations registry. Schema composition via $ref becomes the standard way to express shared fields (e.g. a common 'authorship.schema.json' imported by every block schema).

CLOSURE (agent/step-8.5-orchestrator 2026-05-09T23:58:13.827Z): Structurally closed by `899bcc3` (Step 4 6 shared enum schemas + schema-migrations.ts + schema-validator $ref resolution) + `a4e8c9c` (Step 5.1 15 registry schemas $id+version + 3 $ref consolidations). 23 schemas (8 framework + 15 registry) carry $id (pi-context://schemas/<name>) + version: '1.0.0'; schema-migrations.ts ships per-schema migration registry + runMigrations chain; schema-validator pre-registers + resolves $ref. gaps.title: Mandates as a typed block in the substrate contract
gaps.description: Under the unifying heuristic (substrate-arc-frame.md §1.1), every typed context that gets composed into a prompt belongs to one canonical contract. Mandates today live as ~/.claude/mandates.jsonl — a flat JSONL store with id/title/rule/tags fields, prefixed all-or-nothing on every UserPromptSubmit by an external claude-side hook. Under the heuristic mandates become a typed block at user scope with their own schema, enabling: selective composition (lens views like _lens:mandates-for-classify inject only classifier-relevant mandates rather than all-or-nothing), schema-validated writes (no parallel ungated path for mandate authoring), supersession edges (one mandate retiring another via closure-table relations), authorship attestation (mandates gain the same FGAP-004 target as project blocks). Concrete schema fields needed beyond today's id/title/rule/tags: scope (user/project/agent — couples to DEC-0010), priority/severity, applicability_conditions (which agents, which contexts — couples to FGAP-010 applicability predicate language), lifecycle (active/superseded), supersedes (lineage). Surfaced as gap #35 in the heuristic-widening pass.

evidence.reference: Today's mandate store: flat JSONL, no schema, no canonical write surface, no per-context selective composition. The all-or-nothing prefix on UserPromptSubmit is the degenerate 'all mandates' lens.
Heuristic-widening pass surfaces mandates as in-scope under the same heuristic that governs project blocks.

gaps.impact: Without typed mandate blocks: mandate authoring bypasses block-api (parallel ungated path); mandate-relevant lens injection (selective composition by context) is impossible; mandate authorship attestation has no target; supersession lineage cannot be carried; agent-scope opt-out / opt-in is unreachable. The heuristic-widening's second-class citizen problem: mandates exist in prompt-composition reality but not in the substrate contract.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: policy-as-data; declarative governance store; typed user-scope substrate
gaps.proposed_resolution: Land mandates.json + mandates.schema.json under the configured user-scope root (per DEC-0010). Schema fields: id, title, rule, tags (preserved from today), plus scope, priority, applicability_conditions, lifecycle, supersedes. Migrate ~/.claude/mandates.jsonl content via one-shot conversion. Update the UserPromptSubmit hook to read from the new typed store (or to apply lens projection rather than all-or-nothing prefix). Resolution is gated on DEC-0007 (composition contract) for applicability_conditions evaluation timing and DEC-0010 (scope layering) for the user-scope discovery anchor.

gaps.title: Monitor specs as typed blocks under the substrate contract
gaps.description: Monitor specs today live as `.pi/monitors/<name>.monitor.json` with ad-hoc shape (classify.agent + collectors), classify templates as `examples/<name>/classify.md` with handcoded `{% if %}` guards on context variables. Monitor write-actions bypass block-api (issue-065 — silent schema drift from destination blocks). Under the unifying heuristic monitor specs become typed blocks: monitor.schema.json captures the spec contract (collectors, classify routing, write actions); validateMonitor() lives in pi-project SDK as a peer to validateProject() and validateRelations(). Classify templates declare typed contextBlocks (per DEC-0008) flowing through the same composition layer (DEC-0007) as workflow agents. Surfaced as gap #36.

evidence.reference: Bundled monitor specs and classify templates use ad-hoc shape; no monitors.schema.json exists.
executeWriteAction in monitor write-action path bypasses block-api per issue-065 — silent schema drift surface.
Heuristic-widening pass surfaces monitor specs as in-scope typed contexts.

gaps.impact: Without typed monitor blocks: monitor authoring lacks schema-validated writes (issue-065's parallel ungated path stays open); monitor classify templates duplicate composition logic that lens injection now provides; validateMonitor() does not exist (issue-039 mirrors this); monitor tooling (issue-038) cannot route through canonical surfaces. Monitors as a class become second-class citizens of the substrate.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: typed monitor specifications; declarative observation store; canonical monitor authoring surface
gaps.proposed_resolution: Land monitors.json (flat block) or monitors/ subdirectory (only if DEC-0009 enacts subpath form, which is the rejected direction under the heuristic) + monitors.schema.json + validateMonitor() in pi-project SDK. Monitor write-actions route through block-api appendToBlock for any destination block they target (closes issue-065). Classify templates adopt typed contextBlocks (DEC-0008). Resolution coupled to DEC-0007 (composition contract for classify-prompt assembly) and DEC-0009 (storage primitive direction).

gaps.title: Applicability predicate language for typed-context entries
gaps.description: Under the composition contract (DEC-0007) each typed-context entry (lens view, mandate, monitor output, raw block) carries an applicability declaration: which agent / classifier / context the entry applies to. Today this is implicit in template authoring (`{% if conversation_history %}`), in agent YAML inputSchema, and in monitor classify routing. Under the unifying heuristic applicability becomes a first-class declarative field with a predicate language that the composition layer evaluates at compile-time (or per-turn) to decide what enters the prompt. The predicate language must express: which agent kinds (workflow agents vs classifiers vs query agents); which scopes (user / project / agent); which lifecycle states (open issues only, enacted decisions only); which tags or attributes; logical composition (AND/OR/NOT). Surfaced as gap #38.

evidence.reference: compileAgent's contextBlocks loop today reads each declared block unconditionally; no applicability filter at the compile boundary.
Classify templates use {% if %} guards on Nunjucks vars to express applicability — implicit, per-template, not declarative.
Heuristic-widening pass identifies applicability as the channel by which selective composition resolves which typed contexts enter which agent prompts.

gaps.impact: Without an applicability predicate language: composition contract (DEC-0007) cannot specify when each typed-context entry applies; mandate-block applicability_conditions (FGAP-008) and monitor-spec applicability (FGAP-009) lack a shared expression form; lens-view filters (gap #30, collapsed into DEC-0008) lack a runtime evaluator. Each typed-context kind would invent its own ad-hoc applicability surface — the same fragmentation the substrate arc retires.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: declarative applicability predicates; pre-composition filter expressions; typed-context selectivity
gaps.proposed_resolution: Specify an applicability predicate mini-language (or adopt an existing one — couples to ADR-0004's expression-language comparison; CEL is a candidate). Predicates live as a typed field on every typed-context entry's schema (mandates, monitor specs, lens views). Composition layer (DEC-0007) evaluates predicates at compile-time against {agent_kind, scope, context_tags, ...}. Predicates fail closed: missing predicate means 'applies always'; failed evaluation means 'does not apply.' Resolution gated on DEC-0007 for evaluation timing semantics.

gaps.title: Staleness engine for research blocks
gaps.description: Research entries (`.project/research.json`, schema enacted with R-0001..R-0011 in canonical substrate) carry grounding metadata (version-pinned dependencies, retrieved revisions, dated external refs) and stale_conditions (e.g. 'pi-ai upgrades past 0.63.x'). The schema declares a `stale` lifecycle state, but no engine currently fires stale_conditions against the actual state of installed dependencies, git revisions, or external-URL content hashes. Staleness must be checked by hand. There is also no propagation primitive: when a research entry transitions to stale, its `informs` edges to decisions, features, reviews, gaps, and plans cannot fire structured diagnostics flagging any enacted artifact whose supporting research has gone stale. The gap was reserved in `analysis/research-blocks-design.md` but never filed canonically until now. issue-061 is the mirror entry that tracked the work pending this filing.

evidence.reference: Schema declares `stale_conditions` array per entry, `grounding` block carrying version-pinned dependencies and retrieved revisions, and `stale` as a lifecycle state in the research-entry status enum. The substrate is in place; the engine that consumes it is not.
11 research entries (R-0001..R-0011) carrying stale_conditions; none can be automatically evaluated.

evidence.lines: lifecycle section (planned → in-progress → complete → stale | superseded) and stale_conditions / grounding / informs design
evidence.reference: Original design specifying the staleness-engine surface; the gap was reserved here but not filed in canonical substrate prior to this entry.
Mirror entry tracking the unfiled gap pending this filing.

gaps.impact: Research entries declare stale_conditions but the framework cannot fire them automatically — staleness detection is manual and ad-hoc. Downstream invalidation cannot propagate: an enacted decision whose grounding research has gone stale receives no diagnostic; a feature whose research basis has decayed cannot be flagged. Research's value as ground truth substrate (per analysis/research-blocks-design.md) is materially weakened: entries accumulate without an authoritative-vs-stale distinction. This compounds with each new research entry and each external dependency upgrade.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: grounding decay; citation rot; evidence expiry; staleness propagation
gaps.proposed_resolution: Land a staleness-engine runtime in pi-project (or expose via project-sdk) that: (1) reads every research entry's `stale_conditions`; (2) evaluates each condition against the current state — installed package versions for pi-ai/dependency conditions, git revisions for repo-scoped conditions, content hashes or HEAD checks for URL-scoped conditions; (3) transitions matching entries to `status: stale` via canonical block-api updateItemInBlock; (4) walks `informs` edges to emit structured diagnostics for any enacted decision / feature / review / gap whose supporting research has gone stale. Schedule: on-demand via SDK call; optionally cron-driven (couples to the loop / scheduled-tasks surfaces) when those land. Predicate language for stale_conditions couples to FGAP-010 (applicability predicate language) — a shared expression layer would unify both.

gaps.title: No canonical write-surface for schema files
gaps.description: pi-project's block-api provides typed write-surface for block data (.project/<name>.json) via writeBlock / appendToBlock / updateItemInBlock / appendToNestedArray / updateNestedArrayItem / removeFromBlock / removeFromNestedArray / readBlockDir. No parallel surface exists for schema files (.project/schemas/<name>.schema.json). Every schema modification — adding an enum value, adding a property, restructuring — happens via filesystem-direct Edit, bypassing the write-time-validation pattern block-api enforces for block data. This is a parallel-ungated path relative to the F-006 mitigation pattern: schemas are foundational artifacts of the substrate contract but lack the typed write surface that block files have.

evidence.reference: Nine exported write functions all route through blockFilePath() to .project/<name>.json. None target .project/schemas/<name>.schema.json. No writeSchema, addSchemaEnumValue, addSchemaProperty, or equivalent exports.

Conventions section reads 'All schemas are user-customizable. Edit `.project/schemas/*.schema.json` to add fields, change enums, or restructure — no code changes needed.' This implicitly acknowledges Edit as the only path; there is no tool-mediated alternative.

Modified via filesystem Edit on 2026-05-03 to add 'superseded' to the status enum + corresponding x-lifecycle states/transitions update. Edit was the only canonical path because no block-api schema-write surface exists. The schema gap motivated this entry.

gaps.impact: Every schema edit is a manual operation outside the block-api validation pattern. Schema validity (well-formed JSON Schema, AJV-compilable) is unverified at write time. Cross-block invariants — e.g., that an enum-value removal is rejected when existing block items still carry that value — are not enforced. Schemas drift independently of the items they validate. Schema modifications cannot participate in the block-validation snapshot/rollback contract because they happen below the block-api boundary.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: schema-write surface; meta-validation; schema-as-substrate write contract
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Extend block-api with schema-write functions: writeSchema (full replacement with AJV meta-validation), addSchemaEnumValue (additive enum extension with check that no existing items become invalid), addSchemaProperty (additive property extension with check on required-key handling), removeSchemaEnumValue (with rejection if any existing item still carries the value). All functions perform JSON Schema meta-validation at write time and check that existing block items remain valid under the modified schema. SDK validateProject gains schema-modification atomicity guarantees. Block-validation rollback extends to cover schema files in its snapshot/restore set.

gaps.title: Lens-of-lenses composition — current LensSpec cannot express cross-block aggregation
gaps.description: Current LensSpec (packages/pi-project/src/project-context.ts) requires a single string `target` block and a single `relation_type`. There is no shape for a lens that aggregates multiple blocks (issues + decisions + features in one view) or that composes other lenses (a roadmap-shaped aggregation that pulls grouped output from named child lenses). This blocks every roadmap-shape, plan-shape, or any user-facing summary view that needs to span block kinds. The user's stated capability goal — 'LLMs can create roadmaps and plans out of blocks' — cannot be expressed under the current LensSpec without a flat-lens scope reduction (which is mandate-004 violating).

evidence.reference: LensSpec interface declares target: string (single block); relation_type: string (single edge label); no field for composing lenses or pulling from multiple blocks.
loadLensView reads exactly one block via lens.target; renderLensView emits one heading-per-bin; no codepath for multi-target or lens-id resolution.
lenses items require target as string; no kind/composition/targets/members fields. additionalProperties: false rejects any extension.

gaps.impact: Every aggregation that spans block kinds requires either (a) a manual-edit flat lens reducing the user's hierarchical design intent (scope reduction), or (b) hand-written prose substitution for what should be a substrate primitive. Until this gap closes, roadmaps, plans, cross-cutting summary views, status rollups across mixed blocks, and any future lens variant (filter, join, etc.) all stall. Forward concern surfaced by the substrate-arc proposal: introducing kind: 'composition' as the first lens-shape variant means subsequent variants (filter, join) will need a discriminated-union schema if not consolidated; FGAP-012's resolution should specify the discriminator field shape so future variants extend cleanly rather than fork.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: lens composition; cross-block aggregation; discriminated-union lens variant
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Extend LensSpec with kind: 'target' | 'composition' (discriminator), targets: string[] (when composition; pulls items from multiple blocks), members: array of either { lens: <id> } (sub-lens reference) or { from: <block>, where: <field-equality> } (flat selection). config.schema.json oneOf-enforces the kind-discriminated union. resolveComposition(cwd, lens) SDK function recurses sub-lens references with cycle detection (composition_cycle_detected diagnostic). loadLensView dispatches on lens.kind. renderLensView emits per-member subsections for composition lenses. validateRelations gains lens-graph cycle detection on relation_type='lens_member'. Closes the substrate-shape gap that the roadmap/plan substrate proposal demands.

LensSpec extended with kind/targets/members; resolveComposition with cycle detection; loadLensView dispatches on lens.kind. gaps.title: No canonical status vocabulary registry — status rollup hardcoded in TS, not user-extensible per project
gaps.description: The substrate has no canonical mapping from per-schema status enums to a normalized status vocabulary. Each block schema independently declares its own status enum (issues uses open|resolved|deferred; decisions uses open|enacted|superseded; tasks uses todo|in_progress|completed). Any aggregation across blocks (status rollup over mixed-kind items in a roadmap phase or plan) must collapse these to a normalized set (complete|in_progress|blocked|todo|unknown). Hardcoding the mapping in TypeScript (the proposal's STATUS_VOCABULARY) is not user-extensible per project and bakes in canonical-engineering vocabulary the user has flagged as opaque. Forces every cross-block aggregation to fail open or to lose semantic precision when a project introduces a new status value.

evidence.reference: issues.status enum: open|resolved|deferred
decisions.status enum: open|enacted|superseded (3-state lifecycle, x-lifecycle metadata)
tasks.status enum: todo|in_progress|completed|cancelled (different vocabulary)

gaps.impact: Without a canonical mapping at the substrate layer, every consumer that aggregates status across block kinds (status rollup, roadmap phase status, plan status, dashboards) must either ship its own hardcoded mapping (duplicated drift surface) or fall back to per-block reporting (no rollup possible). Per-project status extensions (a project adds a 'queued' status) cannot be onboarded without code change. Sub-gap surfaced by the roadmap/plan substrate proposal that needs first-class substrate handling.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: status vocabulary registry; status normalization; cross-block status rollup
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Add status_buckets field to config.schema.json: { complete: string[], in_progress: string[], blocked: string[], todo: string[] }. Each bucket lists the per-schema status values that map into it. Per-project extensible — a project adds 'queued' to todo by editing config.json. SDK rollupPhaseStatus / rollupPlanStatus functions read from getProjectContext(cwd).config?.status_buckets with a default mapping (resolved/done/enacted/completed → complete; in_progress/active → in_progress; etc.) if absent. Validator emits status_unknown_value warnings for items whose status doesn't appear in any bucket.
