gaps.title: Lens composition cannot express edge-traversal rollups — only field-filtered item-set unions
gaps.description: The lens `composition` kind (config.schema.json lenses[].members) unions members that are each either a sub-lens reference or a {from, where} field-equality item-filter; resolveComposition (project-context.ts) walks those members and unions the resulting item-sets. There is NO lens construct for walking closure-table edges to assemble a hierarchical/containment rollup (e.g. feature_contains_story then story_contains_task, or phase→task). Surfaced in unit 2.2 (DEC-0037 packaged conception, commits b8a9d7e B0 / a4c5257 B4): the intended `work-rollup` starter lens (feature→story→task containment projection) could not be authored because the composition shape cannot express edge-traversal and lenses[] had zero precedent to validate an edge-rollup shape against. Shipped the 2 field-derived status lenses (tasks-by-status, gaps-by-status) and deferred work-rollup. Note: the edge-traversal PRIMITIVES already exist (walkDescendants / edgesForLens / walkLensDescendants in project-context.ts + lens-view.ts) — the gap is that the lens-SPEC has no construct that consumes them for rollup projection. Intended outcome: a lens construct expressing edge-traversal rollups (walk one or more relation_types to a max_depth; nest/group items by the traversal), so containment hierarchies become first-class lens projections — completing DEC-0037 starter-lens coverage for composite/rollup kinds.

evidence.reference: lenses[] composition members = sub-lens ref OR {from, where} field-equality filter; no edge-traversal/relation_type-walk member shape
resolveComposition unions field-filtered member item-sets; walkDescendants/edgesForLens edge-traversal primitives exist but are not consumable from a lens spec for rollup projection
lenses[] ships 2 field-derived status lenses; work-rollup edge-traversal lens absent (deferred at unit 2.2 B0)

DEC-0037 (each sample packaged with a starter lens) — incomplete for composite/rollup kinds without an edge-traversal lens construct; DEC-0013 (relations are edges — the surface a rollup lens would traverse)

gaps.impact: Containment/rollup projections — the dominant show-a-feature's-full-work-tree and show-a-phase's-tasks views — are not expressible as lenses. DEC-0037 starter-lens coverage is field-derived-only; composite kinds (feature/phase) get no rollup projection. Blocks authoring the work-rollup sample lens.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a lens construct that expresses edge-traversal rollups (walk declared relation_type(s) to a max_depth from a root block, nesting/grouping items by the traversal), backed by the existing walkDescendants/edgesForLens primitives. Implementation specifics intentionally NOT prescribed (outcome-only); the implementer should survey the existing closure-table traversal surface (project-context.ts walkDescendants/edgesForLens, lens-view.ts walkLensDescendants) + reuse them rather than add a parallel traversal. Once landed, author the deferred work-rollup sample lens (feature_contains_story → story_contains_task) in samples/conception.json.

gaps.title: block_kind canonical_id rename requires a file/data_path/array_key/schema_path cascade — unsupported by renameCanonicalId (deferred from FGAP-060/unit 2.3)
gaps.description: The FGAP-060 renameCanonicalId engine (commit 33b76a7, unit 2.3) supports 4 canonical_id kinds (item, relation_type, lens, layer) and THROWS on kind=block_kind. block_kind canonical_id rename is entangled with the block data-file basename, data_path, array_key, and schema_path: buildIdIndex (project-sdk.ts) resolves loc.block by the data-file basename, while config.invariants[].block / lenses[].target / lenses[].targets[] / lenses[].members[].from / hierarchy[].parent_block / hierarchy[].child_block reference the block_kind canonical_id. Renaming the canonical_id alone (config references only) produces canonical_id != basename, breaking buildIdIndex prefix-vs-block invariant + invariant/lens block-resolution. A correct block_kind rename is a coupled filesystem cascade: rename <old>.json -> <new>.json + schemas/<old>.schema.json -> schemas/<new>.schema.json + config.block_kinds[].{canonical_id,data_path,array_key,schema_path} + every config reference, keeping canonical_id == basename. Rare by design (DEC-0023: display_name is the relabel surface; block_kind canonical_id rename is near-never for settled conceptions). Intended outcome: extend renameCanonicalId with a block_kind cascade path (file + schema + array_key + config references, atomic) OR canonicalize block_kind canonical_id immutability post-authoring.

evidence.reference: renameCanonicalId throws on kind=block_kind (unsupported — filesystem cascade)
buildIdIndex resolves loc.block by data-file basename, decoupled from config.block_kinds[].canonical_id — the coupling a block_kind rename must keep consistent
DEC-0035 names block_kind canonical_id as a primary-key the engine serves; DEC-0023 makes display_name the cheap relabel surface; DEC-0013 edge model

gaps.impact: A block_kind canonical_id cannot be renamed through tooling; if ever needed it is a manual multi-file cascade. Low practical impact — display_name handles relabeling and block_kind canonical_id rename is rare for settled conceptions.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Either extend renameCanonicalId with a block_kind path that performs the data-file + schema-file + array_key + config-reference cascade atomically, OR canonicalize block_kind canonical_id immutability post-authoring (display_name only). Implementation specifics NOT prescribed (outcome-only); survey the data_path/array_key/schema_path/basename coupling first.

gaps.title: Full-state derivation capability (currentState) — derive the project's complete state from the substrate as an extension capability exposed dual-surface; do not hand-maintain it
gaps.description: Zero-loss (DEC-0040, as amended 2026-05-21) requires the project's CURRENT STATE (focus / per-arc status / in-flight / next-action / blocked) to be a pure function of the SUBSTRATE — an EXTENSION capability, exposed via the dual surface (Pi tool + scripts/orchestrator/*) to BOTH the Pi runtime and the Claude Code harness (consumed exactly as filing is). FGAP-059 derives only what-is-next. UN-CONFLATION (DEC-0040 amendment): the earlier 'projection engine that regenerates MEMORY.md/HANDOFF/Claude-tasks/plan' framing conflated two layers — those are CLAUDE CODE HARNESS-owned files, not extension projections; the extension's job is to EXPOSE currentState, and the harness APPLIES it to refresh its own files (a harness workflow/skill, not extension code). CORE LANDED: sub-unit 2.6A (commit 0cbd676) shipped currentState(cwd) -> { focus, inFlight, nextActions, blocked } + the context-current-state Pi tool + scripts/orchestrator/current-state.ts (dual surface), reusing projectState + roadmap-plan topoSort/resolveStatusVocabulary; verified on the live .project. REMAINING: atomic-next refinement — v1 ranks open gaps by priority+id + unblocked tasks by topo, but is not active-arc-aware (it surfaces all open P1 gaps, not 'the one next thing'); enrich to weight the active arc / in-flight phase. Subsumes FGAP-059.

evidence.reference: projectState derives partial state; no full focus/next/in-flight derivation; validateProject does not project to consuming surfaces
TASK-043 in-progress (superseded in-place migration) + TASK-044 planned (rename engine actually delivered) — stored state contradicts reality
FGAP-059 atomic-next derives only next-action, not full state

gaps.impact: State drift = loss, violating the zero-loss goal. A fresh session is misled before it reads anything else: MEMORY.md auto-loads a wrong + truncated focus. Execution truth survives only in one hand-maintained gitignored file (HANDOFF).
gaps.proposed_resolution: currentState(cwd) deriving complete state from the substrate, exposed dual-surface (Pi tool + orchestrator script) — usable from the Claude Code harness as filing is; the harness applies it to refresh its own working-memory files (harness-side, not extension-written, per DEC-0040 amendment). Core delivered in sub-unit 2.6A. Remaining (outcome-only): refine atomic-next to be active-arc/in-flight-aware rather than priority+id over all open gaps. Reuse projectState + roadmap-plan topo/status; do not parallel them. Extends FGAP-059.

gaps.title: Unit-of-work closure produces no enforced substrate record (DEC-0018/0020 unenforced) + validateProject lacks cross-block status-drift checks
gaps.description: Two coupled enforcement holes that let process/state leak out of the substrate (zero-loss goal, user 2026-05-21). (1) DEC-0020 mandates every work-unit produce typed substrate blocks (TASK/VER); DEC-0018 mandates runtime-demo + adversarial verification per step. Both are unenforced: this session three substantial units (2.1b config-declared invariants, 2.2 packaged conception, 2.3 rename engine) landed as commits + Claude Code tasks + HANDOFF prose but produced NO TASK/VER blocks (units 2.1b/2.2 have no task at all; 2.3 = TASK-044 left planned). The process-of-work is therefore not reconstructable from the substrate alone. (2) validateProject has no cross-block status-consistency check: nothing flags that TASK-044 is planned while its FGAP-060 is closed and the rename engine is shipped, or that TASK-043 is in-progress for a DEC-0036-superseded approach. The substrate cannot self-detect the lying-state condition. Intended outcome: (a) a unit-closure gate — a unit is not done until its TASK + VER (+ any DEC) are filed; (b) validateProject cross-block status-drift diagnostics (task vs its referenced gap/feature status; completed-task vs verification; task describing superseded work) so the substrate surfaces its own drift.

evidence.reference: DEC-0020 (units produce typed substrate blocks) + DEC-0018 (verify per step) — mandated but unenforced; units 2.1b/2.2/2.3 violated both
TASK-044 planned while FGAP-060 closed + engine shipped; TASK-043 in-progress for superseded in-place migration — undetected drift
validateProject has edge-integrity + config-invariants but no cross-block status-consistency (task<->gap/feature/verification) checks

gaps.impact: Process-of-work leaks out of the substrate (zero-loss violation); the substrate cannot self-detect status drift, so stale state goes silently uncaught until a human audit. Feeds the state-derivation gap (FGAP-072): derived state is only as honest as the statuses it reads.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Enforce unit-closure substrate records (TASK+VER per unit, per DEC-0018/0020) + add validateProject cross-block status-consistency diagnostics. Implementation specifics NOT prescribed (outcome-only). Lands under DEC-0040; statuses it validates feed FGAP-072 state derivation.

gaps.title: Residual pi-project-era 'project' naming debt across pi-context source/surface — package is pi-context + dir .context (DEC-0029) + surface /context, but source identifiers, files, Pi tools, and the command still say project
gaps.description: The 2026-05-08 rebuild renamed the package pi-project -> pi-context; DEC-0029 set the substrate dir to .context and named a forthcoming /context subcommand surface; FGAP-066 designs /context init onboarding. But the SOURCE still carries pi-project-era project naming throughout (~1079 occurrences in packages/pi-context/src): 6 project-*.ts files (project-sdk, project-context, project-dir + tests), 3 package.json subpath exports (./project-sdk, ./project-context, ./project-dir), ~13 Pi tool names + the /project command + customTypes (project-status, project-init, project-validate, project-validate-relations, project-edges-for-lens, project-walk-descendants, project-roadmap-load/render/validate/list, project-add-work, project-lens-curate), 12 exported project* identifiers (projectState, projectRoot, projectDir, validateProject, ...), and cross-package consumers in pi-jit-agents / pi-workflows / pi-behavior-monitors that import the ./project-* subpaths. This is INTERNAL-NAMING inconsistency debt, NOT a DEC-0025 vocabulary-neutrality violation: DEC-0025 governs the user-substrate CONCEPTION vocabulary (block kinds / relation types / statuses, derived from config), whereas project-sdk / projectState / /project are the framework own module + tool identifiers. The substrate-DIR name (.project via config.root) is config-data per DEC-0015 (resolveContextDir; the .project literal already removed from source per FGAP-035) and flips to .context at cutover (DEC-0029) — a separate concern from this source-identifier debt. Intended outcome: rename the project->context source identifier surface (project-sdk.ts -> context-sdk.ts; project-context.ts / project-dir.ts -> context equivalents; projectState/projectRoot/projectDir/validateProject -> context or substrate equivalents; /project -> /context command; project-* -> context-* Pi tools; ./project-* -> ./context-* subpath exports; + every cross-package consumer import + tests) so code matches the package / dir / surface name. OPEN sub-decision (settle first, worth a DEC): the target-naming scheme — e.g. project-context.ts is itself awkward (context-context); candidates context-core / context-substrate; projectRoot/projectDir -> contextRoot/contextDir; validateProject -> validateContext or validateSubstrate. Mechanism: a source-wide codemod across all four packages + tool/command registrations + package.json subpaths + consumer imports + tests — DISTINCT from the FGAP-060 renameCanonicalId engine (which renames SUBSTRATE canonical_ids, not source code). Until this FGAP it was captured only as HANDOFF re-derivation step-4 prose + the plan file (a DEC-0040 single-source violation this filing corrects).

evidence.reference: SDK named project-sdk despite pi-context package / .context dir / /context surface; exports projectState/validateProject/buildIdIndex etc.
./project-sdk, ./project-context, ./project-dir subpath exports (consumed cross-package) carry the old name
~13 project-* Pi tool names + /project command + customTypes

DEC-0029 (.context dir + forthcoming /context surface) — code lags it; DEC-0025 (vocabulary-neutral) is a DISTINCT concern (user vocab, not framework identifiers); DEC-0040 (single-source) — this rename was prose-only until now

gaps.impact: Code/surface name mismatch (pi-context package + .context dir + /context surface vs project-* source) confuses adopters and every fresh LLM, and the /context surface (FGAP-026 Phase 6 + FGAP-066 onboarding) cannot land cleanly without it. Was untracked in substrate (HANDOFF/plan prose only) — a DEC-0040 violation.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Settle the target-naming scheme (open sub-decision; candidate a DEC), then codemod the project->context source identifier surface (files / exports / Pi tools / command / package subpaths / cross-package consumers / tests) to match package + dir + surface. Outcome-only; not the FGAP-060 substrate canonical_id engine (this is source code). Sequenced as re-derivation step 4 (extension refactor), after step 3 re-populate + before/with cutover (step 5) — exact sequencing the user's call.

gaps.title: No edge-write Pi tool — in-pi agents cannot create DEC-0013 relations through the extension tool surface
gaps.description: For any harness-confined in-pi agent (workflow step, monitor, dispatched agent, or a tools-only Pi on a fresh install) restricted to extension-provided tools, there is no registered Pi tool to create a DEC-0013 relation (edge). relations.json is a bare top-level JSON array of {parent, child, relation_type, ordinal?}; the generic append-block-item / update-block-item tools target data[arrayKey] and therefore cannot append to a bare array (empirically: appendToBlock against relations throws 'must be array'). Edge creation exists only as the library writeRelations (project-context.ts) plus the orchestrator CLI (scripts/orchestrator/migrate-canonical-id.ts) plus the rename engine internals — none exposed as a Pi tool. So the foundational primitive of the whole model (an inter-item relationship) is unreachable through the tool surface. Intended outcome: an edge-write Pi tool, the dual-surface twin of writeRelations.

evidence.reference: tool enumeration: relation tools are all read-only (project-validate-relations, project-edges-for-lens, project-walk-descendants, walk-ancestors, find-references); no edge-write / append-relation tool is registered
writeRelations exists as a library surface (consumed by the rename-canonical-id engine + orchestrator migrate-canonical-id.ts) but has no pi.registerTool twin

relations is a bare top-level JSON array (no array_key), so generic append/update-block-item (which operate on object[arrayKey]) structurally cannot write edges; empirical probe: appendToBlock on relations fails 'must be array'
DEC-0013 (edges are THE inter-item relationship surface; FK-as-field forbidden) + DEC-0019/0020 (every substrate op = library + Pi tool + CLI script as a unit) — the Pi-tool leg is the missing one

gaps.impact: In-pi agents cannot author relationships: the .context re-population (DEC-0036 step 3), any tool-only consumer, and any in-pi workflow/monitor that needs to relate items are all blocked from the core primitive. The DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface is broken precisely at DEC-0013, the model's foundation. Sharpest of the three write-twin gaps — not even a degenerate path exists (append-block-item structurally rejects the bare-array shape; only write-block whole-file replace could blind-overwrite relations.json, unvalidated).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register an edge-write Pi tool as the dual-surface twin of writeRelations — append a single edge {parent, child, relation_type, ordinal?} (and/or a bulk variant), routed through the canonical write path with the same atomicity (tmp+rename) and DispatchContext attestation as the item-CRUD tools. Guard semantics (relation_type registered in config.relation_types; endpoints resolve via buildIdIndex; cycle detection) either enforced at write or deferred to validateProject — implementer to choose per existing precedent (validateRelations already centralizes these checks). Implementation specifics outcome-only; survey the pi-sdk surface + sibling pi extensions for the canonical tool shape. DEC-0013 + DEC-0019/0020.

gaps.title: No config-write Pi tool — in-pi agents cannot author or amend config.json (framework vocabulary)
gaps.description: There is no registered Pi tool to author or amend config.json — the framework vocabulary: block_kinds, relation_types, lenses, invariants, status_buckets, display_strings, layers, installed_schemas/blocks. read-config is read-only; project-init does not scaffold config.json at all (defaults/ ships neither config.json nor config.schema.json — empirical: a fresh-init read-config returns null and validateProject is vacuously clean because there is nothing to validate). writeConfig exists as a library surface (project-context.ts, consumed by the orchestrator + the rename engine) with no pi.registerTool twin. So a tools-only in-pi agent cannot register a block_kind / relation_type / lens / invariant, nor set a config.status_buckets override (DEC-0025) — the entire config-driven machinery (DEC-0015/0025) is unestablishable and unrevisable from inside Pi.

evidence.reference: only read-config registered (read-only); no write-config / amend-config tool
writeConfig library surface exists (orchestrator + rename engine consume it); no Pi-tool twin
project-init scaffold omits config.json + config.schema.json; empirical fresh-init: read-config = null, validateProject vacuously clean

DEC-0015 (config-driven substrate dir + vocabulary) + DEC-0025 (vocabulary-neutral; config.status_buckets override) + DEC-0019/0020 (dual-surface) + DEC-0038 (onboarding) — the Pi-tool twin is missing

gaps.impact: Framework vocabulary cannot be created or revised via tools; onboarding (DEC-0038) — which must elicit and write config — has no write surface; config-dependent tools (project-validate, context-current-state, lenses, roadmap, gather-execution-context) degrade to empty/vacuous on any substrate an in-pi agent provisioned. Blocks the consumption arc (FGAP-068 onboarding rewire) from completing on the tool side.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register a config-write Pi tool, the dual-surface twin of writeConfig — AJV-validated against config.schema.json, supporting scoped amendments (add or replace a single relation_type / block_kind / lens / invariant / status_bucket entry) in addition to whole-config write, with atomic write + DispatchContext attestation. Outcome-only; survey the pi-sdk + sibling pi extensions for the canonical shape; coordinate with the onboarding ceremony (DEC-0038), its primary consumer. DEC-0015 + DEC-0019/0020 + DEC-0025.

gaps.title: No schema-write Pi tool — in-pi agents cannot define new block kinds or evolve existing schemas
gaps.description: There is no registered Pi tool to define a new block kind's schema or evolve an existing one. read-schema is the only schema tool (read-only). writeSchema / updateSchema / readSchema exist with AJV meta-validation (schema-write.ts) plus a per-schema migration registry (schema-migrations.ts), but have no pi.registerTool twin. So a tools-only in-pi agent cannot extend the substrate's type surface — every new block kind or schema version must be authored through the orchestrator CLI or by hand. project-init scaffolds only the legacy defaults/ schema set; there is no tool path to add a schema beyond it.

evidence.reference: only read-schema registered; no write-schema / update-schema tool
writeSchema/updateSchema/readSchema library surface with AJV meta-validation — no Pi-tool twin
per-schema version-to-version migration registry exists; a schema-evolution tool must honor it (version bump + MigrationFn registration)
DEC-0019/0020 (dual-surface) + DEC-0023 (schema title/display_name) + schema-versioning ($id + version + migration) — Pi-tool twin missing

gaps.impact: New block-kind definition and schema evolution are orchestrator-CLI-only; in-pi agents and tool-only consumers cannot extend or migrate the type surface. Onboarding a custom block kind (DEC-0038) and any in-pi schema authoring are blocked. Completes the trio of write-twin dual-surface gaps with FGAP-075 (edge-write) + FGAP-076 (config-write). Lower frequency than edge/config operations (hence P2) but required for full tool-side substrate self-definition.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register schema-write Pi tool(s) — the dual-surface twin of schema-write.ts (write-schema + update-schema) — with AJV meta-validation and migration-registry awareness (version bump + MigrationFn registration on evolution). Outcome-only; survey the pi-sdk + sibling pi extensions for the canonical shape. DEC-0019/0020 + DEC-0023 + schema-versioning.

gaps.title: No tool-surface for MigrationFn registration — breaking schema evolution requires a code change
gaps.description: The FGAP-077 write-schema tool can create/replace a block-kind JSON Schema (incl. bumping its `version`), but it cannot complete a BREAKING evolution. When a schema's `version` changes, existing block items retain the old `schema_version`, and read-time `validateBlockWithMigration` (schema-validator.ts) THROWS a version mismatch unless a `MigrationFn` is registered. A `MigrationFn = (data:unknown)=>unknown` (schema-migrations.ts:45) is TypeScript CODE registered in-source via `registry.register(entry)` — it cannot be carried by a Pi tool param. So a harness-confined in-pi agent (or any tools-only consumer) can write an evolved schema but cannot forward-migrate the existing items through the tool surface; the evolution requires a code change. This is the deferred half of FGAP-077's migration boundary, surfaced + proven by its migration-boundary test. Mirrors the FGAP-071 precedent (unit 2.3 deferred the block_kind-rename cascade it could not cover).

evidence.reference: MigrationFn = (data:unknown)=>unknown registered in-source via registry.register — code, not data; no tool param can carry a function
validateBlockWithMigration throws on schema.version !== block.schema_version when no MigrationRegistry supplied
migration-boundary test: write-schema replace with a bumped version SUCCEEDS, then validateBlockWithMigration THROWS on an old-version item; only a code MigrationFn closes it
DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface — the migration-registration leg is inherently code, no tool/CLI twin possible without an executable-code-carrying surface

gaps.impact: Breaking schema evolution (item-shape change) is not completable via the tool surface — the schema JSON is writable, but existing items are left un-migratable until a developer registers a MigrationFn in source. Onboarding / in-pi agents that evolve a schema must hand off to a code change. Bounds what FGAP-077's write-schema can deliver end-to-end.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only (implementation deferred): provide a path for forward-migrating existing block items when a schema version changes that a harness-confined agent can drive — e.g. a declarative/data-expressible migration spec the framework can interpret (vs an arbitrary code MigrationFn), OR an explicit accepted-boundary doc that breaking evolution is a developer-code task with a clear handoff. Survey whether common migrations (add-optional, rename-field, drop-field) are expressible as data the framework runs, reserving code MigrationFn for the irreducible cases. Coordinate with the schema-write tool (FGAP-077) + onboarding (DEC-0038).

gaps.title: Schema-path surface inconsistency — write/read resolver divergence (config.root) + no path-traversal sanitization on schemaName
gaps.description: Two pre-existing flaws in the schema-file path surface, surfaced during FGAP-077 exploration (NOT introduced by it; the write-schema unit reuses the existing writeSchema path to stay internally consistent). (1) RESOLVER DIVERGENCE: schema-write.ts resolves the schema file via projectRoot(cwd) (project-context.ts — honors config.root); but read-schema (the Pi tool + CLI) and validateBlockWithMigration resolve via schemaPath(cwd,name) -> resolveContextDir(cwd) (project-dir.ts — IGNORES config.root). They coincide in the default substrate (the .pi-context.json pointer dir == config.root) but DIVERGE if config.root is set to a different dir: a schema written by writeSchema/write-schema could then be unreadable by read-schema and unseen by migration validation. (2) NO PATH-TRAVERSAL SANITIZATION: readSchema/writeSchema/schemaPath all interpolate the raw `${schemaName}` into the file path (e.g. `${name}.schema.json`) with no validation — a schemaName like '../../etc/x' escapes the schemas/ dir. With the FGAP-077 write-schema tool now exposing schema writes to harness-confined/in-pi agents, the unsanitized name is a write-side escape, not just read.

evidence.reference: schemaWritePath -> projectRoot(cwd) (honors config.root); raw `${schemaName}` interpolation, no sanitization
projectRoot: returns config.root when set, else resolveContextDir
read-schema resolves via schemaPath -> resolveContextDir (project-dir.ts), ignoring config.root — the divergent read path
validateBlockWithMigration also resolves via resolveContextDir, not projectRoot — same divergence

gaps.impact: (1) A non-default config.root silently splits where schemas are written vs read — write-schema writes a file read-schema/validation cannot find. (2) An unsanitized schemaName can write or read outside the schemas/ dir; now reachable from the tool surface (FGAP-077). Both are latent correctness/safety issues across the whole schema-path surface (read + write together), not isolated to one function.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only: unify the schema-file path resolver across read + write (one resolution honoring or ignoring config.root consistently — decide which is canonical and route readSchema/writeSchema/schemaPath/validateBlockWithMigration through it) AND add schemaName validation (reject path separators / traversal) at the single resolution point so both read and write are guarded together. Fix the surface as a unit; do not patch one function.

gaps.title: Orchestrator CLIs catching transitively-thrown package errors via `instanceof` are module-instance-fragile — prefer name-based checks
gaps.description: An orchestrator CLI that imports an error class from `@davidorex/pi-context/<subpath>` and catches it with `instanceof` fails SILENTLY when the error is thrown TRANSITIVELY through a different package subpath. Surfaced in FGAP-077: scripts/orchestrator/write-schema.ts imported BootstrapNotFoundError from @davidorex/pi-context/project-dir and caught it with `instanceof`, but writeSchemaChecked (from @davidorex/pi-context/schema-write) throws BootstrapNotFoundError via schema-write's OWN internal project-dir module instance — under tsx/symlink resolution that is a DIFFERENT class object, so `instanceof` returns false and the catch falls through to the wrong exit code (uninitialized-cwd hit exit 5 instead of the contracted exit 1). Confirmed empirically: instanceof === false while err.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError' and err.constructor.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError'. Fixed in write-schema.ts with a name-based check. Siblings read-schema.ts / read-config.ts happen to work because they throw the class DIRECTLY from their own project-dir import (same instance) — but the pattern is fragile: any future CLI catching a transitively-thrown package error via instanceof will silently misbehave.

evidence.reference: FIXED here: instanceof BootstrapNotFoundError -> err.name === 'BootstrapNotFoundError' (module-instance-proof); comment documents why
uses instanceof BootstrapNotFoundError — works ONLY because the throw is direct (schemaPath from the same project-dir import); latent fragility if its throw path ever becomes transitive
same instanceof pattern, same direct-throw dependency
BootstrapNotFoundError sets this.name='BootstrapNotFoundError' (project-dir.ts:53) — the name survives the module-instance split that defeats instanceof

gaps.impact: Silent exit-code / control-flow wrongness in orchestrator CLIs when a package error is caught via instanceof across the package boundary under tsx. Currently only write-schema.ts was affected (fixed); read-schema/read-config are correct today but one refactor of their throw path away from breaking. A cross-cutting convention gap, not a single bug.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only: establish + apply the convention that orchestrator CLIs (and any cross-package consumer) catching a pi-context error class check by `err.name === '<ClassName>'` (or a shared type-guard helper exported from the package) rather than `instanceof`, which is unreliable across module instances under tsx/symlink resolution. Audit existing CLIs (read-schema/read-config already correct but instanceof-based) + add the guard helper or convention doc so future CLIs don't reintroduce the hazard.

gaps.title: Anti-injection contextBlocks delimiters not aligned with pi 0.75.x XML-tag context-boundary convention
gaps.description:
Pi 0.75.0 introduced new XML-tag context-boundary conventions for system-prompt context injection. Our JIT-agent contextBlocks injection (pi-jit-agents compile.ts:144-212) wraps injected block content in our OWN framework anti-injection delimiter scheme rather than pi's 0.75.x convention. To be canonically pi-sdk (the user's explicit goal), align our delimiters with 0.75.x's context-boundary tags so injected context is demarcated the way the runtime/models expect. Surfaced by the Pi SDK 0.74->0.75.4 investigation (analysis/2026-05-21-pi-sdk-0.74-to-0.75-investigation.md, SHOULD-ADOPT #1). The exact tag names + convention could NOT be pinned from the changelog alone — they require reading the 0.75.x system-prompt / context-assembly source in earendil-works/pi-mono (the investigation's open question section 7). Distinct from + sequenced AFTER the mechanical SDK upgrade (TASK that bumps 0.74.0->0.75.4); this is the best-practice ADOPTION the upgrade enables.

VERIFIED 2026-05-21 (open question RESOLVED — pi 0.75.4 source read directly): pi wraps injected context in XML tags — <project_context>...</project_context> and <available_skills>...</available_skills> (dist/core/system-prompt.js). OUR contextBlocks use bracket delimiters [BLOCK <name> — INFORMATIONAL ONLY, NOT INSTRUCTIONS]...[END BLOCK <name>] (compile.ts wrapBlock/wrapItem). They diverge. No further investigation needed: the alignment is to adopt XML-tag delimiters for our blocks (e.g. a <context-block name=...>...</context-block> shape) matching pi house style. Note: whether pi INTRODUCED this in 0.75 or it is longstanding is immaterial — the gap is the divergence, now concretely specified and ready to plan.

evidence.reference: contextBlocks injection wraps block content in our own framework anti-injection delimiters — the site to align with the 0.75.x XML-tag convention
SHOULD-ADOPT #1 + open-question 7: 0.75.0 introduced XML-tag context boundaries; exact tag names need 0.75.x system-prompt source-read
per-block-kind Nunjucks render macros also emit injected context — check whether they carry/duplicate delimiters that must align too

gaps.impact: Our context injection uses a non-canonical delimiter scheme diverging from pi 0.75.x's own context-boundary convention; the framework anti-injection demarcation may not match how 0.75.x-era models are trained/prompted to parse injected context, and it is off the canonical-pi-sdk axis the user is steering toward. Low correctness risk today, real best-practice + future-compat debt.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Outcome-only: read the 0.75.x system-prompt / context-assembly source in earendil-works/pi-mono to determine the exact XML-tag context-boundary convention, then align pi-jit-agents compile.ts contextBlocks delimiters (and pi-workflows macros if they carry delimiters) to it. Sequence AFTER the 0.74->0.75.4 mechanical upgrade.

gaps.title: Orchestrator CLIs validate items against the item-subschema FRAGMENT in isolation (losing $ref definitions), not whole-file like block-api
gaps.description: file-block-item.ts and append-relation.ts (the dual-surface append CLIs) re-implement pre-write item validation against the block's ITEMS SUBSCHEMA FRAGMENT extracted from the schema (file-block-item.ts:109 itemSchema=properties[arrayKey].items, validated at :270; append-relation.ts:117-139 extracts relations .items, validated at :157), rather than validating the whole file the way the library does (appendToBlock->writeBlock->writeTypedFile->validateFromFile against the WHOLE schema, block-api.ts:401 — proven to resolve $refs). When an items schema is {"$ref":"#/definitions/<kind>"} (features.schema.json:10, spec-reviews.schema.json:38), the fragment cannot resolve #/definitions/<kind> (parent definitions out of scope) -> --dry-run errors "can't resolve reference". ACTIVE on file-block-item filing features/spec-reviews; LATENT in append-relation (relations.schema.json has no $ref today but the same fragment-in-isolation pattern is shipped). NOTE: the REAL (non-dry-run) file-block-item append delegates straight to appendToBlock and is NOT broken — only --dry-run (this gap) + --auto-id (FGAP-083) fail. Conception (samples) + registry schemas use no item $ref, so .context library filing is unaffected. Root: orchestrator-CLI validation is a PARALLEL re-implementation diverging from block-api whole-file validation (feedback_no_parallel_ungated_paths).

evidence.reference: --dry-run validates item against extracted .items fragment, not whole schema; $ref-to-definitions item fails to resolve
same fragment-in-isolation validation pattern (latent — relations.schema has no $ref yet)
the canonical whole-file validateFromFile path the CLIs should route through (resolves $refs)
items {"$ref":"#/definitions/feature"} — the shape that breaks fragment validation; spec-reviews.schema.json:38 same

gaps.impact: file-block-item — the canonical append surface (CLAUDE.md filing pattern) — cannot append/preview/auto-id features (or any future $ref-item block kind), forcing a non-canonical raw appendToBlock tsx-eval fallback that bypasses the CLI's --writer/--dry-run/--auto-id ergonomics. Undermines the DEC-0019/0020 canonical-script surface for $ref-using schemas; the conception (samples) schemas may use $ref similarly, so .context filing would hit this too.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Both append CLIs should validate the candidate item by composing a whole-block wrapper and running validateFromFile against the FULL schema (mirroring writeBlock/appendToBlock) for --dry-run, NOT the item-fragment in isolation. Best: route --dry-run through the same library validation the real path uses (single path; eliminates the divergence).

gaps.title: file-block-item --auto-id cannot read the id pattern from a $ref item schema (reads itemSchema.properties.id.pattern, absent on a $ref fragment)
gaps.description: file-block-item.ts --auto-id allocates the next id by reading the id PATTERN from the extracted item subschema's properties.id.pattern (file-block-item.ts:110-119 idProp = itemSchema?.properties?.id; pattern at :112). When the items schema is {"$ref":"#/definitions/<kind>"} (features.schema.json:10), the fragment has NO top-level `properties` (they live in the unresolved definition), so idProp is undefined and :157 throws 'Cannot auto-allocate ID: pattern undefined not parseable'. Distinct fix-surface from FGAP-082 (that is the dry-run VALIDATION fragment bug; this is the AUTO-ID pattern-extraction fragment bug) though same fragment-not-resolved root. Symptom-of-FGAP-082's fragment approach but separable: even with whole-file validation fixed, auto-id must resolve the $ref (or read from the definition) to find the id pattern.

evidence.reference: auto-id reads itemSchema.properties.id.pattern; a $ref fragment has no .properties → 'pattern undefined not parseable'
items {"$ref":"#/definitions/feature"}; the id pattern lives in definitions.feature.properties.id, unreachable from the fragment

gaps.impact: --auto-id is unusable for any $ref-item block kind (features, spec-reviews); the orchestrator must pass an explicit id (or use raw appendToBlock). Will recur for any conception/.context block kind that adopts $ref item schemas.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Resolve the items $ref against the parent schema's definitions before reading properties.id.pattern (or, with FGAP-084, route allocation through a block-api helper that dereferences). One-line-ish once the fragment is dereferenced.

gaps.title: No canonical block-api id-allocation helper — orchestrator scripts re-implement next-id allocation
gaps.description: block-api exposes read/write primitives but NO id-allocation helper (no nextId/allocateId; verified absent across block-api.ts). file-block-item.ts re-implements id allocation inline (file-block-item.ts:156-174: parse prefix+width from the id pattern, scan existing items, increment) as a parallel path. Any other surface needing auto-id (the in-pi append tools, future CLIs) must re-implement or hand-scan. The missing primitive is the allocation-side analog of the validation divergence in FGAP-082: allocation logic lives in the CLI, not the library, so it can't be shared + diverges (e.g. it doesn't dereference $ref item schemas — FGAP-083).

evidence.reference: no exported id-allocation function (only read/write primitives)
inline re-implementation of next-id allocation (prefix/width parse + scan + increment) — the logic that belongs in a shared block-api helper

gaps.impact: id-allocation is duplicated/divergent and not reusable by the in-pi tool surface (the Pi append tools have no auto-id at all). Extracting a canonical allocateId(cwd, block) helper would let file-block-item, the in-pi tools, and future surfaces share one correct implementation (and fix FGAP-083 once).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a block-api id-allocation primitive (e.g. allocateId(cwd, blockName) → next id from the block-kind's resolved id pattern + existing items, dereferencing $ref item schemas) and route file-block-item + any in-pi auto-id through it. DEC-0019/0020 dual-surface: library primitive + CLI + (optionally) Pi tool.

gaps.title: Audit all registered Pi tool description strings for in-pi discovery-readiness
gaps.description: FGAP-044 (list-tools) makes the loaded tool surface introspectable by the harness-confined main LLM (DEC-0014): pi.getAllTools() surfaces each tool's `description` as the PRIMARY discovery signal an in-pi agent reads to decide what to call. The descriptions were authored ad-hoc per tool over the project's life with no shared discovery-readiness rubric — they vary in whether they state purpose, parameter semantics, the substrate side-effect, the dual-surface twin (CLI script), and when-to-use vs when-not. A one-pass audit of every pi.registerTool description across packages/*/src/ against a discovery rubric (purpose / params / effect / twin / when-to-use) is its own scoped concern, deliberately kept OUT of B1 (FGAP-044) which only added the introspection mechanism. Spot-check at B1 found existing descriptions adequate for naming but not uniformly self-describing for an agent with no other context.

evidence.reference: list-tools (FGAP-044) returns ToolInfo.description as the in-pi LLM's primary discovery signal — quality of every registered description now load-bearing for discovery
pi.registerTool description strings authored ad-hoc per tool over project life; no shared discovery-readiness rubric (purpose/params/effect/twin/when-to-use)

gaps.impact: An in-pi agent discovering tools via list-tools may mis-select or fail to find the right tool when a description is terse, omits the substrate side-effect, or doesn't name the CLI twin — degrading the autonomy FGAP-044 was meant to enable. Low severity (descriptions are adequate for naming today; this is uniformity hardening), hence P3.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Define a description rubric (purpose / params / effect / dual-surface twin / when-to-use vs when-not). Enumerate every tool via `grep -rn 'pi.registerTool' packages/*/src/` (canonical census); score each description against the rubric; cross-check SKILL.md (generated). Emit a per-tool gap list + remediated description strings; apply through plan mode per feedback_no_adhoc_dev (each edit is a source change). B-cluster (in-pi tool-surface program) hardening; companion to FGAP-044.

gaps.title: relation_types carry no endpoint-kind metadata — edges can wire wrong-kind endpoints undetected; validateProject checks endpoint existence but never kind
gaps.description: config.relation_types[] items carry only {canonical_id, display_name, category, cycle_allowed} (config.schema.json relation_types, additionalProperties:false). There is no declaration of which block_kind(s) a relation_type may connect. Endpoint kinds are resolved at runtime purely from the actual edge (buildIdIndex maps item-id -> {block}); relation_type endpoint metadata is used NOWHERE. Consequently validateProject (project-sdk.ts:1072-1097) verifies an edge's parent/child IDs EXIST but never that their KINDS are legal for the relation_type — e.g. a task_addresses_gap edge whose child resolves to a decision (not a framework-gap) passes validation. The catalog discovery surface (FGAP-068, DEC-0037 packaged view 'schema + its relation_types') also cannot present a kind's applicable relation_types without this metadata; name-token parsing is not viable (task != tasks, gap != framework-gaps, review != spec-reviews, wildcard 'item'). Surfaced during B2/FGAP-068 planning.

evidence.reference: relation_types items: required [canonical_id,display_name,category]; props [...,cycle_allowed]; additionalProperties:false — no source_kinds/target_kinds
validateProject edge-integrity loop asserts parent/child ids resolve in idIndex; never compares their block-kind to the relation_type
buildIdIndex resolves endpoint kind from the item's home block; relation_type metadata is not consulted

gaps.impact: A structurally wrong edge (legal ids, illegal kinds for the relation) is undetectable by validateProject — a correctness hole in the closure-table model. Blocks DEC-0037 packaged catalog (FGAP-068) from showing per-kind relation_types. Closed jointly with FGAP-068 in B2: add optional source_kinds[]/target_kinds[] (wildcard '*'), author them on conception, and add a presence-gated endpoint-kind check to validateProject (gated so the metadata-less frozen .project stays silent).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add OPTIONAL source_kinds[]/target_kinds[] to the relation_types schema (wildcard literal '*'=any kind; optional so frozen .project/config.json keeps validating; version bump, no MigrationFn for additive-optional). Author all 27 conception.json relation_types per the B2 mapping table. Add a validateProject loop after edge-integrity: resolve parent/child kind via idIndex, look up the relation_type, assert parent-kind in source_kinds && child-kind in target_kinds honoring '*'; GATE on the relation_type declaring at least one of the fields (silent where absent => no false positives on metadata-less substrate). severity error. Implemented as part of TASK-054 (B2).

gaps.title: registry/ + defaults/ dirs retained on disk as AJV test fixtures after consumption-MVP de-canonicalized them; delete pending fixture-repoint + a home for 8 conception-less legacy schemas
gaps.description: The consumption MVP (TASK-055) made samples/ the single canonical catalog: installProject + initProject + generate-skills now read samples/, and registry/ + defaults/ were dropped from the shipped package.json files[] (DEC-0011). But the two dirs were NOT deleted from disk because (a) ~7 test files read packages/pi-context/registry/schemas/*.json directly as AJV fixtures (schema-validator.test, context-contracts.test, execution-context.integration.test, index.test + pi-workflows macros/render-architecture/render-task tests) and (b) 8 schemas live ONLY in registry/ with no samples/ home (architecture, audit, conformance-reference, domain, handoff, plan, project, roadmap — legacy, absent from the going-forward DEC-0037 conception). So registry/ + defaults/ are now dead-but-present: unshipped, unread by production, retained solely as test fixtures + legacy-schema holding.

evidence.reference: 8 schemas absent from samples/: architecture/audit/conformance-reference/domain/handoff/plan/project/roadmap
reads registry/schemas as AJV fixtures (one of ~7 test readers); deletion would break these
files[] no longer lists registry/ or defaults/ (TASK-055 commit a53663e) — dirs ship nowhere but remain in the repo tree

gaps.impact: Dead dirs in the repo tree are misleading (a reader may assume registry/ is still canonical) and the legacy-fixture coupling blocks a clean cutover. Low-urgency (no production path touches them; samples/ is canonical) hence P2. Becomes relevant at the .context cutover (DEC-0036) when the substrate vocabulary is finalized.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Repoint the ~7 registry-fixture tests to samples/schemas (where the kind exists) or to inline fixtures; decide each of the 8 conception-less legacy schemas — either give it a samples/ home (if still wanted in the going-forward conception) or drop it (legacy planning vocab not in DEC-0037). Then delete packages/pi-context/registry/ + packages/pi-context/defaults/. Sequence with the .context cutover; not blocking.

gaps.title: Package-relative path resolution uses import.meta.dirname in 3 sites — undefined under tsx-CJS dist-load; normalize to fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)
gaps.description: import.meta.dirname is undefined when a built dist/*.js (type:module) is loaded under tsx's CJS-interop path (tsx -e / subpath import) — proven during B2 where samples-catalog.ts's module-top import.meta.dirname threw ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE and was fixed to path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) (idiom already at project-context.ts:220/433, samples-catalog.ts:34, schema-validator.ts:47, pi-jit-agents/trace-writer.ts:40). Three sites still use import.meta.dirname: (a) pi-workflows/src/bundled-dirs.ts:14 MODULE-TOP-LEVEL (eager) PACKAGE_ROOT = path.resolve(import.meta.dirname, '..') — same eager pattern that broke samples-catalog (highest risk: throws at import under tsx-CJS); (b) pi-context/src/index.ts:335 installProject samplesRoot (lazy, inside fn); (c) pi-context/src/update-check.ts:20 getInstalledVersion (lazy, inside fn). Lazy sites are prod-ESM-safe but latent under tsx-CJS-if-called; eager bundled-dirs.ts:14 is the genuine risk. Codebase already standardizes on fileURLToPath(import.meta.url) in 5 places.

evidence.reference: PACKAGE_ROOT = path.resolve(import.meta.dirname, '..') EAGER module-top; same pattern that threw in samples-catalog under tsx-CJS
installProject samplesRoot import.meta.dirname — lazy, prod-ESM-safe, latent under tsx-CJS
getInstalledVersion import.meta.dirname — lazy, prod-ESM-safe, latent under tsx-CJS
canonical idiom precedent path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) — target form

gaps.impact: No production breakage today (prod loads dist as native ESM; 2 pi-context sites lazy). Risks: bundled-dirs.ts:14 eager-module-top throws if that pi-workflows module loads via any tsx-CJS-interop path (orchestrator tsx -e / some harnesses) — the exact B2 failure class; and inconsistency with the established idiom invites repeating the B2 defect. P3 — no current failing path, cheap to normalize, removes a latent footgun.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Replace all 3 import.meta.dirname package-relative resolutions with path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) (idiom in 5 existing sites). Prioritize bundled-dirs.ts:14 (eager = actual risk). Add fileURLToPath import from node:url where absent. Verify each touched module resolves its package-relative dir under native-ESM AND a tsx -e import probe. Mechanical, no prod behavior change; route through plan mode per feedback_no_adhoc_dev. Consider a biome guard to prevent reintroduction.
